text: The surgeon begins the operation by creating a preperitoneal pocket, carefully dissecting the thin peritoneal lining from the robust posterior rectus fascia. This meticulous dissection preserves the integrity of both the peritoneal lining and the fascia, ensuring the peritoneal lining will serve as a biological barrier between the mesh and the intestine.

During the dissection, the surgeon discovers another hernia, which is reduced and incorporated into the larger repair. The posterior rectus fascia is fully exposed, facilitating the integration of the additional hernia into the repair process.
The surgeon deploys a fifteen-centimeter piece of mesh into the preperitoneal pocket to cover the large hernia defect, ensuring the mesh is positioned within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a self-retaining suture to close the large abdominal wall hernia, applying three sutures to fully close the defect.

The surgeon unrolls the mesh and positions it centrally over the repaired abdominal wall. The mesh is sutured directly to the posterior rectus fascia to secure it in place and prevent migration. The preperitoneal pocket is closed using an absorbable self-retaining suture.
The mesh is fully concealed within the preperitoneal pocket, providing a protective barrier between the mesh and the intestines, minimizing the risk of adhesions and erosion.
The surgeon removes the needles from the abdomen and proceeds to close the twelve millimeter port site, ensuring the incision is securely sealed.
